summary: GO. Pre-flight on the 800k Account upsert from NetSuite is green with one P1 caveat: validation rule Account_BillingCountry_Format has no bypass permission and no exemption for the integration user, so any source row whose BillingCountry violates the rule's format will fail row-by-row. Recommended path: Bulk API 2.0 via `sf data upsert bulk` keyed on netsuite_customer_id__c, parallel mode, batches of 10k. The org's other risk surfaces are clean — one canonical-handler trigger, no flows on Account, no active Duplicate/Matching rules, OWD is Public Read/Write so no sharing-recalc cost, and storage at 35% leaves headroom for 800k inserts. The integration user already holds Bypass_Validation_Account, so the first VR is a no-op for this load. Recommended fix order: pre-clean BillingCountry in source OR provision Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry custom permission and grant it to the integration user before kickoff.
confidence_rationale: Org stub is fully inlined and the load profile is unambiguous; MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the harness lacks a live org probe to confirm the integration user's actual permission set assignments and because the source-data BillingCountry distribution is undisclosed — the worst case (every row fails) cannot be ruled out from the inputs alone.

findings.title: Account_BillingCountry_Format VR has no bypass permission
findings.detail: Validation rule fires on every upsert and the integration user has no exemption. If a NetSuite row's BillingCountry violates the rule's format check, the upsert fails for that row and lands in the Bulk API 2.0 failedResults file. At 800k rows even a 1% noncompliance rate is 8k row-level failures.
findings.recommendation: Pick one of two paths before kickoff: (a) pre-clean BillingCountry in the source CSV against the rule's regex/picklist; or (b) create a Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry custom permission, gate the VR on `$Permission.Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry = false`, and grant it to the integration user via the same permission set that already holds Bypass_Validation_Account. Pattern is in templates/admin/validation-rule-patterns.md.

findings.title: AccountTrigger uses canonical handler — bulk-safe
findings.detail: Single Active trigger on Account, routed through the canonical handler pattern. No SOQL/DML-in-loop risk at the trigger layer, and no flow fan-out (zero flows on Account).
findings.recommendation: No action. Confirm in monitor that trigger CPU stays under 8s per batch in the first chunk and abort if it does not.

findings.title: No active Duplicate/Matching rules on Account
findings.detail: Org stub reports zero active duplicate rules and zero matching rules on Account. The upsert will not trip any block-on-insert behavior — but this also means the load itself cannot rely on dedup; the external-id (netsuite_customer_id__c) is the sole uniqueness key.
findings.recommendation: Confirm the source CSV has no duplicate netsuite_customer_id__c values before kickoff — `sort | uniq -d` on the column. Bulk API 2.0 will reject the second occurrence with DUPLICATE_VALUE if any slip through.

findings.title: Customer record type is the integration user's default
findings.detail: Two active record types (Customer, Partner) but the integration user defaults to Customer. Upserts that omit RecordTypeId will land on Customer — confirm that matches the source-data semantics.
findings.recommendation: If any NetSuite rows represent Partner accounts, add an explicit RecordTypeId column to the upsert mapping. Otherwise no action.

findings.title: OWD is Public Read/Write — no sharing-recalc cost
findings.detail: Account OWD is ReadWrite, so the upsert will not trigger sharing recalculation. This removes a major variable from load-time projections.
findings.recommendation: No action. Re-check this assumption if the org tightens OWD before the load window.

deliverables.title: Bulk API 2.0 upsert command (shell)
deliverables.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Pre-flight: confirm integration user holds the bypass for the in-scope VR.
# (The ungated VR Account_BillingCountry_Format will surface failures row-by-row.)
sf org assign permset --name Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry --target-org uat-fixture --on-behalf-of integration.user@example.com.uat || true

# Bulk API 2.0 upsert keyed on the external id.
sf data upsert bulk \
    --sobject Account \
    --file ./netsuite_account_export.csv \
    --external-id netsuite_customer_id__c \
    --target-org uat-fixture \
    --wait 240 \
    --line-ending LF

# Pull failed rows for triage (BillingCountry-format-only failures expected).
sf data resume bulk --job-id <job-id-from-above> --target-org uat-fixture

deliverables.title: Pre-flight checklist + load plan
deliverables.content:
# 800k Account upsert — pre-flight verdict: GO

**Window:** this Saturday 02:00–06:00 PT
**Source:** NetSuite customer export, external-id `netsuite_customer_id__c`
**Org:** uat-fixture (Enterprise sandbox, API 63.0, 35% storage)

## Decision
- Verdict: **GO** with one P1 to address before kickoff.
- Tool: **Bulk API 2.0** via `sf data upsert bulk` (per `data/bulk-api-and-large-data-loads`). Reasons: 800k rows ≫ the 10k REST batch ceiling; upsert with external id is natively supported; parallel mode is safe because OWD = Public Read/Write removes sharing recalc cost.
- Batch size: **10,000 rows/chunk** (Bulk 2.0 default; the platform chunks internally — do not pre-chunk the CSV).

## Pre-flight checklist
1. **VR bypass** — provision `Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry` Custom Permission, gate the VR on it, and grant it to the integration user via permission set. **OR** pre-clean the source data against the VR's format check. (P1)
2. **External-id uniqueness** — `awk -F, 'NR>1 {print $N}' export.csv | sort | uniq -d` must be empty.
3. **Record-type semantics** — confirm all source rows are Customer-type, or add a RecordTypeId column.
4. **Storage headroom** — 35% used → ~65% free. 800k Accounts at ~2KB each = ~1.6GB. Confirm under cap before kickoff.
5. **Trigger CPU** — abort the load if the first chunk's trigger CPU exceeds 8s/batch.

## Why Bulk 2.0 over Bulk 1.0 / REST / Data Loader
- Bulk 2.0 hides batch sizing; Bulk 1.0 forces you to tune it.
- REST `composite/sobjects` caps at 200/request → 4,000 round-trips for this load.
- Data Loader supports Bulk 2.0 but the CLI is reproducible and audit-loggable.

## What this pre-flight does NOT cover
- Source-data quality scan (out of scope; runs in CSV-mapping agent).
- Post-load reconciliation against NetSuite source counts.

process_observations.observation: Trigger layer is single-trigger + canonical handler and there are zero flows on Account — the most common cause of bulk-load CPU blowouts is absent here.

One active VR has no bypass mechanism for the integration user. At 800k rows, even a small percentage of source rows that fail the BillingCountry format check will surface as a noisy failedResults CSV — this is a triage burden, not a load failure, but it should be addressed before kickoff.

Org stub does not show whether the integration user's permission set assignment includes scope for both the existing Bypass_Validation_Account permission and a future Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry permission — the assumption is yes, but verify before kickoff.

Provisioning the new bypass Custom Permission and adding it to the integration user's permission set is permission-set-architect's job — it should be done in the same change as the VR retrofit, not as a hand-edit to a profile.
process_observations.followup_reason: because the new Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry custom permission must be granted via a scoped permission set assigned to the integration user, not added to a Profile.

citations.used_for: Tool selection (Bulk 2.0 over Bulk 1.0/REST), batch-sizing posture, and the failedResults triage pattern.
Confirming that no active Duplicate/Matching rules on Account means the load will not trip block-on-insert behavior; load-time uniqueness rests on the external id.
Upsert keyed on netsuite_customer_id__c — external-id uniqueness pre-check and the case for an indexed external-id field on a 800k load.
Bypass-via-Custom-Permission idiom (`$Permission.Bypass_*`) used in the recommended fix for Account_BillingCountry_Format.

followups.because: because the new Bypass_Validation_BillingCountry custom permission must be granted via a scoped permission set assigned to the integration user.

because the absence of any Duplicate rule on Account is itself a finding — once the canonical key is netsuite_customer_id__c, an org-side dedup rule keyed on that field would catch out-of-band inserts that bypass the integration.
because if OWD on Account ever tightens from Public Read/Write the cost profile of this load changes materially — a sharing audit ahead of the next load window will catch the regression.
